plainMeaning: This closing line bows to the awakened ones who know and see everything and who have reached the blissful, deathless, never-returning home of liberation — the Jinas who have conquered all fear.
recitation: savvaṇṇūṇaṁ savvadarisīṇaṁ, siva-m-ayala-m-arua-m-aṇaṁta-m-akkhaya-m-avvābāha-m-apunarāvitti, siddhigaï-nāmadheyaṁ ṭhāṇaṁ saṁpattāṇaṁ, namo jiṇāṇaṁ jiabhayāṇaṁ

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit savva (Skt. sarva, 'all') + nnū (Skt. jña, 'knower') + gen./dat. pl. -ṇaṁ; = Skt. sarvajñebhyaḥ.
wordByWord.notes: Names the kevala-jñāna (omniscient knowledge) of the Arihanta.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit savva (sarva) + darisī (Skt. darśin, 'seer') + -ṇaṁ; = Skt. sarvadarśibhyaḥ.
wordByWord.notes: Paired with savvannū as the standard couple kevala-jñāna + kevala-darśana.
First of the seven epithets qualifying the liberated abode (in the underlying gen. pl. siva… apunarāvittiṁ saṁpattāṇaṁ).
The leading m- is a sandhi consonant inserted between vowels, not part of the root; root = ayala.

wordByWord.etymology: euphonic -m- + arua (Skt. aruja / arujas, 'without sickness'); a-ruja.
wordByWord.notes: Not to be misread as a single word 'maruya'; it is ma(sandhi)+arua.
Evokes the ananta-catuṣṭaya (infinite knowledge, vision, bliss, energy) of the Siddha.
Often listed among the eight qualities of the Siddha (avyābādha-sukha).

wordByWord.gloss: (and) from which there is no return (to rebirth)
wordByWord.etymology: euphonic -m- + a- (not) + punar (again) + āvitti (Skt. āvṛtti, 'returning'); = apunarāvṛtti.
wordByWord.notes: The seventh epithet; declares liberation irreversible — the soul never re-enters saṁsāra.
Forms the compound siddhi-gati with the next token.
siddhi-gaï = the destination that is liberation, the abode at the universe's apex (siddha-śilā).

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. nāman, 'name'; here first member of nāma-dheya.
wordByWord.notes: Read with the following dheyaṁ as the single word nāmadheyaṁ.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. -dheya (from √dhā, 'to place/assign'); nāmadheya = 'that which bears the name'.
wordByWord.notes: Qualifies ṭhāṇaṁ: 'the abode designated by the name siddhi-gati'.
The seven adjectives above all attributively describe this ṭhāṇaṁ.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit saṁpatta (Skt. saṁprāpta, 'fully reached') + gen./dat. pl. -ṇaṁ; saṁ-pra-√āp.
wordByWord.notes: Past participle used substantively: 'of/to the ones who have arrived at that abode'.
Begins the sealing salutation that closes the whole Śakra-stava.
Dative of homage governed by namo.

wordByWord.gloss: to those who have conquered fear
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit jia (Skt. jita, 'conquered', √ji) + bhaya (Skt. bhaya, 'fear') + dat. pl. -ṇaṁ; = jita-bhaya.
wordByWord.notes: Closing epithet; the Jina has overcome every fear (the canonical seven/eighteen bhayas).

literalTranslation: To the all-knowing, to the all-seeing — to those who have reached the abode designated 'siddhi-gati' (the destination of perfection), [an abode] blissful, immovable, free of disease, endless, imperishable, untroubled, and never-returning: reverence to the Jinas, the conquerors of fear.
idiomaticTranslation: Reverence to those who know all and see all, who have arrived at that abode called the State of Perfection — blissful, unshakable, free of every affliction, infinite, imperishable, beyond all pain, the place from which none returns. Homage to the Jinas, who have vanquished all fear.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: After the reflexive couplets just sung (himself crossed-over and ferrier, awakened and awakener), this clause finally names the destination of that whole journey. It pivots in grammar and in mood: it opens with two present, living attributes of the Arihanta — savvannū (all-knowing) and savvadarisī (all-seeing), the kevala-jñāna and kevala-darśana pair — and then, with saṁpattāṇaṁ ('of those who have reached'), shifts to the accomplished terminus, the ṭhāṇaṁ called siddhi-gati. The seven adjectives are not loose praise; each cancels one limitation of saṁsāra. siva removes inauspiciousness, ayala restlessness, arua sickness, aṇaṁta finitude, akkhaya decay, avvābāha pain, and apunarāvitti the very possibility of falling back — liberation is declared irreversible. A point worth hearing aloud: the epithets are strung by an inserted euphonic -m- (makāra-āgama), so the chanter delivers siva-m-ayala-m-arua… in one unbroken breath, the sound itself enacting the seamless, undivided perfection it describes. The clause then seals not only itself but the entire Śakra-stava with namo jiṇāṇaṁ jiabhayāṇaṁ — a return to bare, direct homage after the long garland of titles. jiabhaya ('fear-conqueror') is the fitting final word: the being who has reached the deathless abode is, by definition, the one for whom no fear remains.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse encodes the Jain account of mokṣa precisely. savvannū / savvadarisī assert that omniscience (kevala-jñāna) and total perception (kevala-darśana) are intrinsic to the liberated, the very faculties revealed when the destructive (ghātī) karmas fall away. The seven epithets of siddhi-gati describe the Siddha-state at the apex of the loka (the siddha-śilā): notably apunarāvitti enshrines the distinctively Jain claim that liberation is permanent — there is no descent back into embodiment, unlike cyclic-return models. aṇaṁta points to the ananta-catuṣṭaya (infinite knowledge, vision, bliss, and energy), and avvābāha to the avyābādha-sukha, the painless bliss counted among the Siddha's qualities. jiabhaya names the fruit of fearlessness that the abhaya-daya ('giver of fearlessness') clause earlier promised: the Jina, having destroyed the root passions, has nothing left to fear.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Recited within pratikramaṇa — the very act of 'turning back' from fault — this clause reorients the penitent toward the goal of that turning: a state that is blissful, stable, and beyond return. A reciter can dwell on the seven epithets one breath at a time, letting each negate a present anxiety (instability, affliction, fear of loss), and close on jiabhaya as a vow to cultivate the fearlessness that right conduct slowly yields.

crossReferences.text: Namutthuṇaṁ (Śakra-stava) as embedded in the canon — the homage formula to the Arihantas
On mokṣa and the irreversible nature of the liberated state (Tattvārtha-sūtra, ch. 10)
Parallel petition for the deathless, supreme state — 'samāhi-vara-m-uttamaṁ diṁtu'

sources.title: Ṇamōkāra mantra (canonical homage formula, Ardhamāgadhī orthography and dating)
sources.consultedFor: Background on the Prakrit homage formulae (namo… -āṇaṁ) and the Ardhamāgadhī vs. Maharashtri orthography of namo/ṇamo.

sources.title: Summary of Jain Agams — JAINA
sources.consultedFor: Canonical placement of the praise of the Arihantas within the Śvetāmbara Āgama corpus (Aupapātika and allied sūtras).

sources.title: Siddhashila — the abode of the liberated souls (siddhi-gati)
sources.consultedFor: Doctrine of the siddha-śilā as the deathless abode reached by the liberated, glossing siddhi-gati and apunarāvṛtti.

sources.title: JainQQ digital library (host of Sheth, Pāia-sadda-mahaṇṇavo, Prakrit–Hindi dictionary)
sources.consultedFor: Prakrit glosses of savvannū, savvadarisī, the seven siddhi-gati epithets, the euphonic -m- (makāra-āgama), and jia-bhaya.

notes: The leading 'm' on ayala, arua, aṇaṁta, akkhaya, avvābāha and apunarāvitti is a euphonic hiatus-breaking consonant (makāra-āgama), not part of the roots; hence the printed મરુઅ is ma+arua, NOT a single word 'maruya'. The seven epithets of siddhi-gati are counted as printed (siva, ayala, arua, aṇaṁta, akkhaya, avvābāha, apunarāvitti); some editions enumerate or split them slightly differently. Book orthography preserved verbatim, including word-internal spacing (સિવ મયલ મરુઅ…, મવ્વાબાહ મપુણરાવિત્તિ) and સવ્વન્નૂ with double-n. IAST renders Prakrit forms (e.g. apunarāvitti, avvābāha); the Sanskrit chāyā equivalents (apunarāvṛtti, avyābādha) are given in etymology only.
